Issue: Wishlist additions are not populating the "Your Favorites" side panel — product-page heart state and favourites dropdown are out of sync
Issue description:
Detailed Description: Adding a product to the wishlist via the heart icon on a product page correctly updates the product-page UI (heart turns red/filled and the button's accessible label changes to "Remove from wishlist"), but the "Your Favorites" side panel opened from the global nav heart icon always renders the empty-state message, regardless of how many items have been added. The two surfaces appear to be reading from different sources of truth — the per-product heart state is persisting somewhere (likely local state, localStorage, or the product component's own store), while the favourites panel is either reading from a separate store, a not-yet-hydrated store, or an endpoint that is returning an empty list.

Repro steps:

  1. Go to any product page, e.g. https://thehealios.com/product/vitamin-d3.
  2. Click the outlined heart icon next to the price to add the product to the wishlist.
  3. Observed (good): the heart fills in red, and the button's accessible label updates to "Remove from wishlist" — confirming the add action was registered at the product-card level.
  4. Click the heart icon in the top-right of the global navigation (labelled "Favorites").
  5. Expected: the "Your Favorites" side panel slides in and lists Vitamin D3 4000 IU Gummies with its image, name, price, and a "Remove" / "Add to bag" affordance.
  6. Actual: the panel renders the empty-state copy: "You haven't added any favorites yet. Browse our collection and click the heart icon to save items you love." — even though the product clearly has its heart active in the background.

Suggested investigation path (for the engineer):

  • Verify both the product-page heart component and the Favorites panel read from the same wishlist store / context / hook. If there are two, consolidate.
  • Check whether the favourites panel subscribes to store updates (e.g. it may only read on mount and not re-hydrate when new items are added while the panel is closed).
  • If a backend endpoint powers the panel (e.g. GET /api/wishlist), confirm the mutation that the heart-button triggers on click is actually hitting the correct endpoint and that the GET response contains the new item afterwards. A stale cache key on a data-fetching library (SWR/React Query) is a common culprit — invalidate on mutation.
  • Confirm behaviour for both logged-in and anonymous sessions (the wishlist should persist to server-side for logged-in users and to localStorage for guests, and the panel should merge/read from whichever is authoritative).
  • Test removal parity: removing from the product page should also remove from the panel (and vice versa) without a manual refresh.

Acceptance criteria:

  • Adding a product from any product page or product-card heart icon causes it to appear in the "Your Favorites" side panel without a page refresh.
  • Removing a product from either surface removes it from the other without a refresh.
  • The panel's empty-state only shows when there are genuinely zero items in the wishlist.
  • Behaviour is consistent for logged-in users and guest sessions.
  • The heart-icon counter/badge in the top nav (if any) reflects the correct count.

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Vitamin D3 product page with wishlist active: Shows the Vitamin D3 4000 IU Gummies PDP with price R283.42 (strike-through R333.44) and a filled red heart icon immediately to the right of the price. The heart's accessible label is "Remove from wishlist" — definitive proof that the product is in the wishlist state from the product-page component's perspective.
  • Screenshot B — "Your Favorites" side panel open on the same page, at the same moment: Slide-in panel on the right titled "Your Favorites" with a close (×) button, and the body text "You haven't added any favorites yet. Browse our collection and click the heart icon to save items you love." The Vitamin D3 item visible in the background (still showing its filled red heart) is absent from the list — this is the inconsistency the ticket documents.

Where the reviewer can find this visually:

  1. Open https://thehealios.com/product/vitamin-d3 (or any product detail page).
  2. Click the outlined heart icon to the right of the price — confirm it fills red and the hover/aria label flips to "Remove from wishlist".
  3. Click the heart icon in the top-right of the global header (to the left of the settings cog and account icons) — the "Your Favorites" drawer slides in from the right.
  4. Observe the empty-state copy despite the active heart on the product in the background.

Webpage URL(s):

  • Product page used to reproduce: https://thehealios.com/product/vitamin-d3
  • Bug is present on any product detail page under the /product/* pattern and on any product-card heart in listing views (e.g. https://thehealios.com/category/all).

  • Screenshot A — Product page with filled wishlist heart next to price: ss_5524ygqqw
  • Screenshot B — "Your Favorites" side panel showing empty-state despite active wishlist item: ss_9338d4id5

Issue: Define the Shipped → Delivered transition when Healios is not integrated with a courier — options for manual mark, customer-confirm CTA, and future webhook integration
Issue description:
Detailed Description: The admin Orders view has a Delivered tab, but there is currently no mechanism by which an order actually gets into it. Healios is not yet integrated with any courier/logistics partner (dispatch is flexible and mixed — drop-offs, ad-hoc carriers, whoever is cheapest/fastest at the time), so the "when is an order delivered?" signal has no authoritative source. This ticket is a discovery/design ticket: pick an approach (likely a layered one) that works in both the pre-integration and post-integration worlds. The reviewer has deep logistics experience and should be the decision-maker on which options to adopt.

Current state:

  • Shipped tab has 1 order (Monique One, HLS-2026…); Delivered tab shows "No orders found"; there is no button or control anywhere on an order row to mark it as delivered, no automated job inferring delivery, and no courier integration populating it.

Proposed options (to be evaluated by the reviewer — not all need to ship at once):

  1. Manual "Mark as delivered" by admin (MVP, zero-integration).
    • Add a CTA on Shipped orders: "Mark as delivered" (date-pickable — admin can set actual delivery date rather than "now" for back-filling).
    • Triggers the existing 03-delivery-confirmation template (currently "Preview only" — will also need wiring, same pattern as ticket #4a).
    • Records who marked it and when on the order audit trail.
    • Good for the current low-volume, hands-on fulfillment model.
  2. Customer-side "Confirm my order arrived" CTA (complementary, improves data quality).
    • In the shipping-confirmation email and on the customer-facing order page, surface a button: "Did your order arrive? Confirm delivery."
    • On click, the order auto-transitions to Delivered and optionally opens the Post-purchase / Review request lifecycle (11-post-purchase template, currently "Preview only").
    • Handles the common case where the customer knows first.
    • Requires a secure one-click token link from the email (magic link) so it works without login.
  3. Heuristic auto-transition (fallback, low confidence).
    • X days after the Shipped event with no other signal, auto-flip to Delivered with a flag "inferred" on the record.
    • Useful for keeping the Delivered tab honest for reporting, but should be clearly distinguishable from confirmed deliveries in the UI (e.g. dotted border or "Assumed delivered" badge).
    • Reviewer's logistics experience should set the threshold (domestic vs international, carrier class, etc.).
  4. Future: courier webhook / tracking-API integration (post-partnership).
    • Once Healios commits to one or more preferred carriers (Aramex, DHL, The Courier Guy, etc.), subscribe to each carrier's tracking webhooks or poll their APIs.
    • On the authoritative "Delivered" event from the carrier, auto-transition and fire 03-delivery-confirmation with the carrier's proof-of-delivery timestamp and (if available) recipient name / signature image.
    • Design the data model today so the Shipment details record (from ticket #3) can carry carrier_code, tracking_number, webhook_subscribed: bool, last_carrier_event, and proof_of_delivery_url — this way we don't need a migration later.

Suggested path forward (for discussion):

  • Ship (1) Manual mark as delivered now so the Delivered tab becomes usable.
  • Ship (2) Customer-confirm CTA in the same milestone as ticket #4a's email wiring, so the shipping email has the CTA baked in from day one.
  • Keep (3) off unless reviewer approves — inferred data in reports is risky.
  • Treat (4) as a separate epic once a preferred carrier is selected.

Acceptance (for the MVP subset, options 1 + 2):

  • Shipped orders have a visible admin CTA to mark as delivered (with date picker).
  • The shipping-confirmation email includes a "Confirm delivery" magic-link CTA for the customer.
  • Either action transitions the order to Delivered, lands it in the Delivered tab, and fires 03-delivery-confirmation (once wired live).
  • Per-order audit trail records which pathway moved the order to Delivered (admin_manual | customer_confirm | future: carrier_webhook).

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Delivered tab empty state: Orders page with the Delivered (0) tab active, showing "No orders found". Confirms no order has ever reached Delivered and no mechanism exists to move one there.
  • Screenshot B — Status tabs header showing the gap: Same page, tabs row: Pending (0) / Processing (0) / Shipped (1) / Delivered (0) / Cancelled (3). The Shipped → Delivered gap is the subject of this ticket.
  • Screenshot C — Email Templates page showing 03-delivery-confirmation template: Exists in the Transactional group, marked "Preview only" — needs wiring to whichever trigger(s) are chosen from the options above.

Where the reviewer can find this visually:

  1. Admin sidebar → Orders → click the Delivered (0) tab. Note the empty-state and the absence of any "how do orders get here?" affordance.
  2. Admin sidebar → Orders → click the Shipped (1) tab → expand the Monique One row. Note the absence of any "Mark as delivered" CTA on the order detail.
  3. Admin sidebar → Email Templates → Transactional group → 03-delivery-confirmation card, "Preview only".

Webpage URL(s):

  • https://thehealios.com/admin/orders (with Delivered tab active)
  • https://thehealios.com/admin/emails

  • Screenshot A — Delivered tab, empty state: ss_4815wendg
  • Screenshot B — Status tabs with Shipped(1) / Delivered(0) gap: ss_35057oqji
  • Screenshot C — 03-delivery-confirmation in Preview only: ss_1059h4veh

Issue: No customer email is dispatched when an order is transitioned to "Shipped" — 02-shipping-confirmation template exists but is not wired to the status-change trigger
Issue description:
Detailed Description: Advancing an order to the Shipped status in the admin fires no email to the customer. The transactional template 02-shipping-confirmation already exists in the template library but is marked "Preview only" — it has never been wired to a live trigger — so the Shipped status is currently a silent, admin-only state change.

Repro steps:

  1. Test customer Monique One (thehealiosteam+1@gmail.com), order HLS-2026…, was advanced Pending → Processing → Shipped in the admin Orders view.
  2. Expected: a shipping-confirmation email lands in the customer's inbox the moment the order flips to Shipped (or, per ticket #3, when the admin clicks the "Mark as ready for shipment & notify customer" CTA after capturing courier/tracking data).
  3. Actual: 36+ minutes after the status change, no email has been received. Inbox and spam both empty for post-confirmation messages.

Scope of this ticket (narrow — dispatch only, tracking-capture is ticket #3):

  • Promote 02-shipping-confirmation from "Preview only" to Live and bind it to the order.status_changed: shipped event.
  • Ensure it is dispatched exactly once per transition into Shipped (idempotent on order_id + status).
  • Until ticket #3 ships, the email should gracefully degrade: render without the tracking block if courier/tracking fields are empty, while still confirming "your order is on its way" with an order summary.
  • Once ticket #3 ships, this email becomes the carrier of the "Track your order" primary CTA, the selectable tracking number, and the "Copy tracking number" secondary action.
  • The copy should follow the logistics framing agreed in ticket #3: "allocated for shipment and leaving shortly" rather than "has shipped right now" — since the courier's own tracking events are the authoritative in-transit source.

Acceptance:

  • Moving an order to Shipped in admin delivers an email within normal transactional latency.
  • The "Preview only" chip on the template card is replaced with the black Live chip.
  • The email is captured in the per-order notifications timeline (see ticket #2) showing dispatch time and recipient.

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Orders list with the Shipped order: Monique One / HLS-2026… with the purple Shipped chip, £0.03, 36 minutes ago. Demonstrates the order has been in Shipped status long enough that the email should have dispatched and been received if the trigger were wired.
  • Screenshot B — Email Templates page: 02-shipping-confirmation card showing the "Preview only" chip, confirming the template is not bound to a live event.

Where the reviewer can find this visually:

  1. Admin sidebar → Orders → Shipped (1) tab → the Monique One row — note the elapsed "36 minutes ago" timestamp with no corresponding inbox delivery.
  2. Admin sidebar → Email Templates → Transactional group → 02-shipping-confirmation card — note the "Preview only" chip instead of "Live".
  3. Customer inbox (thehealiosteam+1@gmail.com) — search for Healios shipping-related messages; only the initial order-confirmation email is present.

  • https://thehealios.com/admin/emails

Issue: Add courier & tracking capture on Processing orders, with a "Ready for Shipment" action that transitions to Shipped and triggers the shipping email with tracking CTA
Issue description:
Detailed Description: The admin today can walk an order from Pending → Processing → Shipped → Delivered using status chips alone, with no mechanism to capture courier/tracking data and no customer-facing communication of that data. This ticket introduces a proper handoff step between Processing and Shipped that captures the logistics information and uses it to populate the shipping-confirmation email.

Note to the engineer picking this up: the reviewer/owner has deep courier and logistics experience, so the logistics logic can lean on their judgement (multi-parcel, carriers without public tracking URLs, international handoffs, etc.). The spec below is the product intent; implementation details can follow best practice.

Flow & scope:

  1. Processing state — new "Shipment details" panel on the expanded order. While an order is in Processing, the expanded order card (currently showing Order Items / Shipping Address / Discount only) should expose a new block titled "Shipment details" with:
    • Courier / carrier name (free-text or selectable from a managed list of preferred carriers — e.g. Aramex, DHL, The Courier Guy, PostNet, DPD, Royal Mail, etc.)
    • Tracking number (required)
    • Tracking URL (required; admin can paste the carrier-specific deep-link. For carriers without a deep link, allow a generic "tracking page" URL.)
    • Service type / dispatch note (optional free-text, e.g. "Overnight", "Economy")
    • Estimated delivery window (optional date range)
    • Parcel count (optional, defaults to 1; supports multiple tracking numbers if >1)
  2. Validation + primary CTA. Once all required fields are complete, a primary call-to-action button becomes enabled at the bottom of the Shipment details panel. Copy: "Mark as ready for shipment & notify customer" (final label reviewable). Disabled state shows helper text describing which field is missing.
  3. On click of the CTA: the order's status transitions from Processing → Shipped automatically (admin does not have to also click the status chip), the shipment metadata is persisted on the order, and the shipping-confirmation transactional email (02-shipping-confirmation) is dispatched to the customer using the captured data.
  4. Customer email content (revised framing — important): the email should not read "your order has shipped" as if it's physically on a van right now. It should read along the lines of "your order has been allocated for shipment and will be leaving shortly" (copy is reviewable). The rationale is that from this point forward the courier's own tracking emails become the authoritative "in transit / out for delivery / delivered" communications, but Healios has proactively told the customer what's about to happen and has handed them the tracking handle. The email should contain:
    • Courier name and an obvious primary CTA button "Track your order" that deep-links to the tracking URL captured in step 1.
    • The tracking number displayed as selectable text with a "Copy tracking number" button (one-click copy to clipboard) so customers who prefer to paste into a third-party tracking aggregator (e.g. 17track, Parcels.app, their courier's own app) can do so easily.
    • The shipping address summary so the customer can double-check the destination.
    • A short note explaining that further in-transit updates will come from the courier directly.
  5. Admin audit trail. Record on the order: who clicked the CTA, timestamp, which tracking data was captured, and confirmation that the email was dispatched (tie in with the "notifications sent" timeline requested in ticket #2).
  6. Edit-after-dispatch. If tracking data needs correcting after dispatch, allow admin to edit the Shipment details and offer an optional "Resend updated tracking email" button (does not re-fire automatically on every edit to avoid spamming the customer).

Current state (what we see today — the gap):

  • Admin Orders /admin/orders: clicking a row expands it to show Order Items, Shipping Address, Discount — and nothing else. There is no field anywhere for courier name, tracking number, or tracking URL, in any status (Pending, Processing, Shipped, or Delivered).
  • The Monique One test order was able to be moved from Pending → Processing → Shipped with zero tracking data captured and zero customer notification.
  • The transactional template 02-shipping-confirmation exists in Email Templates but is marked "Preview only" — it is not yet wired to a real trigger, and even if it were, it has no tracking payload to render because no UI exists to capture it.

Related work / cross-references:

  • Relies on ticket #2 ("Missing Order Processing email") for the upstream Pending → Processing notification.
  • The same pattern (field capture → CTA → status transition → automated email) should be considered for Shipped → Delivered (likely driven by the courier's delivery webhook or a manual confirmation) using the existing 03-delivery-confirmation template — worth filing as a follow-up ticket.

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Admin Orders page with the Monique One order expanded in "Shipped" status: Shows the HLS-2026… Monique One row with a purple "Shipped" chip, total £0.03, 33 minutes ago. The expanded panel below contains only Order Items (Magnesium Gummies 1 × £321.27), Shipping Address (1 Unit Number, Street Name, City Name, 1235, South Africa), and Discount (FREE2026 −£377.93). The visible absence of any courier name, tracking number, or tracking URL field — despite the order already being in Shipped status — is exactly the issue this ticket addresses.
  • Screenshot B — status tabs header: Same page showing the Pending (0) / Processing (0) / Shipped (1) / Delivered (0) / Cancelled (3) tabs, confirming the order has moved all the way to Shipped without any intervening shipment-capture step.
  • Screenshot C — Email Templates page (/admin/emails): Shows 02-shipping-confirmation in the Transactional group with a "Preview only" chip. This is the template that will become live-wired once this ticket ships, and whose payload will be populated by the new Shipment details panel.

Where the reviewer can find this visually:

  1. Where the missing UI should appear: Admin panel → left sidebar → Orders (/admin/orders). Click the Monique One order row (HLS-2026…, Shipped). The row expands to show Order Items / Shipping Address / Discount. A new "Shipment details" block should be rendered in this expanded panel when the order is in Processing, with the "Mark as ready for shipment & notify customer" CTA at the bottom.
  2. Where the status transition currently happens silently: Same order row — the status chip (Pending → Processing → Shipped → Delivered) can currently be advanced without any field capture or email dispatch. This CTA will replace the "Processing → Shipped" click.
  3. Where the email that gets triggered lives: Admin panel → Email Templates (/admin/emails) → Transactional group → 02-shipping-confirmation card (currently "Preview only"). This is the template that needs new variables for courier/tracking number/tracking URL, the "Track your order" primary CTA button, and a "Copy tracking number" secondary action.

Webpage URL(s):

  • Admin Orders (where the new Shipment details panel + CTA should live): https://thehealios.com/admin/orders

Issue: Missing "Order Processing" automated email — customer receives no notification when order status moves from Pending → Processing
Issue description:
Detailed Description: When an admin advances an order's status from Pending to Processing in the admin Orders view, the customer does not receive any email update. Today the transactional email lifecycle only fires at three moments — confirmation (on checkout), shipping (on "shipped"), and delivery (on "delivered") — leaving a silent gap between "we got your order" and "your order has shipped" that can easily stretch across days. Customers have no way to know their order is actively being prepared.

Repro steps:

  1. Test customer Monique One (thehealiosteam+1@gmail.com) placed an order for Magnesium Gummies. The Order Confirmation email was successfully received and the on-site confirmation page rendered — this step works as expected.
  2. In the admin panel (/admin/orders), the order was advanced from Pending → Processing using the row-level status control.
  3. Expected: the customer's inbox receives an automated "Your order is being processed" email (branded, following the same React Email template family as the other transactional emails). Example copy (reviewable/editable): "Hi Monique — just a quick update: your order is now being processed. We'll send you another email as soon as it ships."
  4. Actual: no email was delivered to the customer's inbox. Checked inbox + spam. The transition happens silently from the customer's perspective.

Root cause observation (from /admin/emails): the Transactional template group contains exactly six templates — 01-order-confirmation (Live), 02-shipping-confirmation, 03-delivery-confirmation, 04-password-reset, 05-account-created, 06-subscription-reminder. There is no order-processing / in-progress template between 01 and 02, so the trigger doesn't exist to be fired.

Requested change — design & wire up an "Order Processing" automated email:

  • New template: 02-order-processing (re-sequence the rest), using the same React Email design system as 01-order-confirmation so branding, header/footer, and variables (customer first name, order number, ETA, items, link to order page) are consistent. Copy is editable; a reviewable starter is: "Hi {{first_name}}, we wanted to keep you in the loop — your order {{order_number}} is now being processed. We'll send another update the moment it ships. In the meantime you can track progress here: {{order_link}}."
  • Trigger logic: fire exactly once when an order transitions from Pending → Processing (manual or automated). Do not re-send on repeated transitions (idempotent by order_id + status).
  • Status-change email matrix (broader principle): every customer-visible status transition should have an associated automated email so the customer always knows the "next step." Suggested matrix:
    • Pending → Processing → send new Order Processing email (this ticket)
    • Processing → Shipped → existing Shipping confirmation (confirm it's wired live; today shows "Preview only")
    • Shipped → Delivered → existing Delivery confirmation (same — currently "Preview only")
    • Any → Cancelled → confirm an order-cancellation email exists or file a follow-up ticket
  • Admin visibility: surface a per-order "notifications sent" timeline on the order detail so admins can see which status emails fired and when (helpful for support).

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Email Templates page, Transactional group: Shows the header "Email templates" with the sub-text "Monique's React Email designs — the same HTML that lands in customer inboxes." Under the Transactional (6) band you can see the six cards in order: 01-order-confirmation (with black Live chip), 02-shipping-confirmation (Preview only), 03-delivery-confirmation (Preview only), 04-password-reset, 05-account-created, 06-subscription-reminder. The visual gap — the absence of any order-processing card between 01 and 02 — is the issue this ticket addresses.
  • Screenshot B — remaining template groups for context: Lifecycle (welcome 1/2/3, abandoned-cart, post-purchase, win-back) and Campaign (product-launch, wellness-drive, restock, promo, bundle) — included to confirm the only logical place for a new "Order processing" template is in the Transactional group.
  • Screenshot C — admin order detail: Same order referenced in ticket #1, showing the status chip "Processing" on the Monique One row (HLS-2026…). This is the state that should have triggered the missing email.

Where the reviewer can find this visually:

  1. The gap (template that doesn't exist): Admin panel → left sidebar → Email Templates (/admin/emails). Under the first section, Transactional (6), scan the cards numbered 01 through 06. Note nothing between 01-order-confirmation and 02-shipping-confirmation — that's the missing template.
  2. The trigger that should fire the email: Admin panel → left sidebar → Orders (/admin/orders). Open the Monique One order row; the status is currently Processing (blue chip). The status was changed from Pending to Processing using the row-level control (or the row "…" overflow menu), which is the action that should have dispatched the missing email.
  3. Proof of missing delivery: Inbox of thehealiosteam+1@gmail.com — search for messages from Healios between the order-confirmation email and now. Only the confirmation email is present; no processing update.

Issue: Admin Order Detail — Currency mismatch: customer-paid ZAR amounts are displayed in GBP (£) without conversion or original-currency reference
Issue description:
Detailed Description: The admin Orders view displays every monetary value with a £ (GBP) symbol, but the underlying number appears to be the raw amount the customer transacted in their own currency (ZAR in this test case). As a result, the admin order detail misrepresents both the currency and the true GBP value of the sale.

Repro steps:

  1. On the live storefront (thehealios.com), currency selector is set to ZAR — Magnesium Gummies is listed at R444.66.
  2. A test customer (Monique One, thehealiosteam+1@gmail.com, shipping to South Africa) placed an order for 1 × Magnesium Gummies using the discount code FREE2026, and successfully received an order confirmation email + confirmation page.
  3. In the admin panel (/admin/orders), the order populates correctly: order number, customer, email, status, date, and status-transition controls (Pending → Processing → Shipped, etc.) all behave as expected — this part is working well.
  4. Opening the order detail reveals the issue: the line item reads "Magnesium Gummies — 1 × £321.27 = £321.27", the discount reads "FREE2026 −£377.93", and the row total reads "£0.03". All values are prefixed with £ even though the customer checked out in ZAR, and the numeric values do not correspond to a real GBP ↔ ZAR conversion of the shop-displayed R444.66.

Two related problems to address:

  • (a) Currency symbol/label is wrong. If the stored amount is in fact the ZAR value the customer paid, it must not be rendered with a £ symbol.
  • (b) No base-currency normalization. If the platform is going to operate on a single base currency (assumed GBP) for reporting, every order must be converted into GBP at the exchange rate at time of order and stored, so admin totals, Dashboard tiles (e.g. "Today £0.03", "Avg Order Value £0.01"), and CSV exports are consistent and meaningful.

Proposed solution (for the admin view specifically — separate from anything the customer sees):

  • Display two values on each order: the transaction currency + amount exactly as the customer paid (e.g. "Customer paid: R444.66 ZAR"), and the base-currency equivalent at the exchange rate captured at checkout (e.g. "Base (GBP): £19.42"). This preserves multi-currency traceability while still allowing clean aggregate reporting.
  • Persist the FX rate used on each order record for auditability.
  • Apply the same fix to: order line item, discount line, order total, Dashboard summary tiles (Today / This Week / Avg Order Value / Needs Action value), and CSV export columns.

Detailed Explanation of Screenshot(s):

  • Screenshot A — Admin order detail (the bug): Shows the expanded "Monique One / HLS-2026… / Processing" row. Under Order Items the Magnesium Gummies thumbnail is listed as 1 × £321.27 with a line total of £321.27. To the right is Shipping Address showing "1 Unit Number, Street Name, City Name, 1235, South Africa" — confirming the destination country is ZA. Underneath is the Discount block with chip FREE2026 and −£377.93. The parent order-row Total column reads £0.03, and the top-of-page tiles read TODAY £0.03, THIS WEEK £0.03, AVG ORDER VALUE £0.01 — all using £ despite the transaction being in ZAR.

Issue: Order Confirmation email has multiple critical defects: wrong order reference, wrong currency, wrong shipping address, broken images, missing discount line, inconsistent fonts, and non-functional footer links
Issue description:
• Title: Order Confirmation email (orders@thehealios.com) — data mismatches with on-site order, broken assets, wrong shipping address, currency error, and footer issues

Defects catalogued

    • Email shows line item £377.96, Subtotal £377.96, Total £0.03 — all in GBP.

  4. Shipping address in email is completely wrong / looks like placeholder/test data

  6. Broken / placeholder product thumbnail next to the line item

  7. Brand logo rendered as plain text "healios" rather than the brand logo image

  8. Social icons shown as plain text boxes "IG", "TT", "YT" instead of actual icons
    • The footer social row renders as plain text abbreviations in grey boxes.
    • Replace with the proper Instagram, TikTok, YouTube icon assets, each linked to the correct Healios channel URL and with accessible alt / aria-label.
  9. Inconsistent fonts / typographic treatment across the email
    • "ORDER CONFIRMED · 24 APRIL 2026" and other all-caps labels are sans-serif tracked.
    • "Thank you, Monique." mixes sans-serif with an italic serif for the name.
    • Body paragraph, line items, totals, and footer all appear to use different weights/sizes that feel off-brand.
    • Standardise on a single brand-approved type system (max 2 families: e.g. display serif + UI sans) with a consistent scale. Italics reserved deliberately, not applied only to the customer name.
  10. Footer "Unsubscribe" link reported as not clickable
    • Per your description, Unsubscribe is not a working link. The screenshot shows it underlined alongside "Manage preferences" and "thehealios.com", but functionally it doesn't navigate.
    • Unsubscribe must be a genuinely clickable, one-click link to a working unsubscribe flow — required for CAN-SPAM / GDPR / POPIA compliance. "Manage preferences" and "thehealios.com" should also both be verified as working links.
    • Recommend also adding the List-Unsubscribe and List-Unsubscribe-Post email headers for one-click unsubscribe in Gmail/Outlook.
  11. Minor: "Track your Order" CTA needs a verified destination
    • The big "TRACK YOUR ORDER" button is present — verify it routes to the correct order-tracking page for this specific order reference and works for both guest and logged-in users.

Acceptance criteria

  1. Order reference is identical across the site, email subject, email body, order history, and admin.

  8. "Track your Order" CTA resolves to the correct tracking URL for the specific order.

Issue: B15 ticket — checkout, signup incentive, and post-purchase account flow
Issue description:
Summary

We're replacing the existing account-creation-at-checkout pattern and the proposed 5% signup incentive with a cleaner flow:

  1. Guest checkout stays open, permanently. No forced account creation anywhere on the purchase path.
  2. The signup popup offers 10% off first order (up from 5%) in exchange for an email address. Marketing consent is a separate, unticked, granular checkbox — decoupled from account creation.
  3. After purchase, the confirmation page shows a single-click "claim your account" prompt that converts the guest order into a password-backed account via an email link (no form to fill in).
  4. Order tracking is always guest-accessible via a tokenised tracking URL emailed in the order confirmation. No login required.

The research that fed this decision lives at /Healios/healios-b15-account-creation-research.docx — read it if you want context on why we rejected forced account creation and why 5% became 10%. You don't need to read it to build the ticket.

Do not swap the live flow until the new flow is fully built and tested. Current logic stays on prod; build the new flow behind a feature flag or on a staging branch, and we'll coordinate the cutover.

Why we're doing this

Three things drove the change:

  • 5% is below the modal category incentive (10%) and gets dwarfed in perception by the existing 15% subscribe-and-save. As scoped, B15 would ship a lever we don't pull hard enough. 10% is the defensible floor and keeps clear headroom below S&S.
  • Forced account creation at checkout costs ~24% of abandoning carts (Baymard). Only brands with a structural excuse (subscription-only, prescription-gated) force it. Healios doesn't have that excuse, and the one UK brand that does force it without justification — Healthspan — gets visibly punished on Trustpilot, with the brand itself incorrectly citing GDPR.
  • GDPR + POPIA compliance wants the discount decoupled from marketing consent. Tying "you must opt in to marketing to get the discount" is the risky construction under both regimes. Keeping them on separate, unticked, granular controls is the cleanest defensible pattern.

What to build

1. Signup popup — "10% off your first order"

Trigger: first-session visitor, delay 10 seconds OR 30% scroll depth OR exit-intent — whichever fires first. Do not re-trigger for visitors who've dismissed (cookie: 30 days) or visitors with an existing email match (returning customers).

Popup contents:

  • Headline: "10% off your first Healios order" (exact copy subject to Monique's sign-off on brand tone)
  • Body: one sentence, benefit-led
  • Single input: email address. No name. No phone. (Huel pattern — minimum fields = highest submit rate.)
  • Unticked checkbox: "Send me marketing emails from Healios. Unsubscribe anytime." — exact wording subject to legal review
  • Primary CTA: "Get my 10% off" — only fires when the checkbox is ticked AND email is valid
  • Secondary dismiss: "No thanks, continue shopping" — visible text link, not just an X. This matters for consent-freely-given.
  • Fine print under the checkbox: a single sentence linking to the privacy policy and stating the code is for first orders only

On submit:

  • Store the email, marketing-consent state, timestamp, source (popup_homepage / popup_product etc.), IP, user-agent in the consent log (see Section 4)
  • Generate a single-use discount code (one per email), value 10% off, minimum basket £0 (no threshold), one-use-per-customer, exclusions: subscribe-and-save orders, bundle SKUs
  • Email the code immediately — welcome email template already exists (emails/lifecycle/07-welcome-1.tsx)
  • If they dismiss via "no thanks" — do not store anything, do not show again for 30 days

Exclusions must be hard-coded: the 10% code must not stack on top of bundle discounts (already up to 20%) or subscribe-and-save (15%). If a customer applies it to a cart containing a bundle or an S&S item, the code silently skips those lines and applies only to eligible SKUs, or rejects with a clear message — your call on UX, whichever is less fiddly.

2. Guest checkout, made prominent

At the account-or-guest step, guest checkout needs to be at least as visible as account creation — same prominence Glossier gives it. Current flow buries it; new flow surfaces it. If Stripe Checkout handles the step natively, confirm the account/guest selection there is equally weighted; if we're on a custom pre-Stripe step, rebuild the button hierarchy.

No fields, no account prompt, no "create an account for faster checkout" upsell at this stage. Just: email → shipping → payment.

3. Post-purchase "claim your account" prompt

On the order confirmation page (/thank-you or equivalent):

  • Confirmation details at the top as now
  • A single new module below: "Save your details — we'll set up your Healios account in one click"
  • One button: "Email me a link to set a password"
  • When clicked, fire a magic-link email to the order email address. Link points to /account/activate?token=.... Token is single-use, expires in 24 hours, signed (JWT or HMAC).
  • At the activate URL: one field (new password), confirm, done. No re-verification of name/address — the order already has those.
  • Account, once activated, is linked to the order record so the customer's order history is populated from the first visit.

If they don't click: nothing happens. The order exists, the customer is a guest, tracking still works via the tokenised URL. Totally fine outcome.

4. Consent log — build this once, use it for everything

We need a durable record of who consented to what and when. POPIA and PECR both require this as evidence if a complaint is ever raised.

Schema (minimum fields):

FieldExample
idUUID
emailjane@example.com
consent_typemarketing_email / marketing_sms / terms_acceptance
consent_giventrue / false
timestampISO 8601 UTC
sourcepopup_homepage / checkout / account_activate / preference_centre
ip_address...
user_agent...
copy_shownthe exact consent text the user saw (store verbatim — if we change the popup wording later, we need to know what the user agreed to)
versionincrementing integer — bump on every copy change

Every time consent is given, withdrawn, or changed, write a new row. Never update an existing one — append-only. This is the single most important compliance primitive.

5. Tokenised guest order-tracking URL

In the order confirmation email, include a "Track your order" CTA that resolves to /orders/track?token=.... Token is:

  • Signed (HMAC with a server secret)
  • Scoped to a single order ID
  • No expiry (order tracking can be needed months later for refunds/returns)
  • Tied to the order's email address — if anyone else gets hold of the link, they can see tracking but nothing else (no password reset, no order editing, no other orders)

The tracking page shows: order status, tracking number + carrier deep link, expected delivery window, "update my address" (if pre-ship), "contact support" link. No login prompt. No "create an account" modal. No popup.

For logged-in customers, /account/orders/:id still works as now.

6. Marketing consent at checkout (not just popup)

At checkout, include the same unticked granular marketing-consent checkbox as a separate step. Wording must match the popup. Reason: customers who dismissed the popup but complete a purchase should still be asked once, at checkout, if they want to hear from us. Both yes and no are fine outcomes — the consent log captures either.

If SMS marketing is a future consideration: leave a commented-out placeholder row for an sms_marketing checkbox in the same component. Eight Sleep's pattern — separate unticked checkbox for each channel, never bundled. Even if we're not launching SMS, build the component so adding it later is a config toggle, not a refactor.

7. Account creation, when it does happen

When an account is created (via claim-account or via a "create account" CTA if we keep one), the legal basis is contract (UK GDPR Art 6(1)(b)) for order fulfilment — not consent. So:

  • Do not show a "by creating an account you agree to marketing" checkbox as part of account creation. Marketing is the separate, earlier popup / checkout decision.
  • Do show a "by creating an account you agree to the terms and privacy policy" checkbox — unticked, links to both. This is terms acceptance, also a separate consent type in the log.
  • Password requirements: minimum 8 chars, not in common-password list (Have I Been Pwned password hash check is the standard — happy to discuss).

Compliance requirements — non-negotiables

This section is the set of things that must be true for the flow to pass GDPR / PECR / POPIA review. Do not "optimise" any of these away in implementation.

  1. Purchase must always be possible at full price without marketing consent. Guest checkout is the mechanism.
  2. All marketing-consent checkboxes must default to unticked. No exceptions. No "we assume you want our emails."
  3. Each consent is granular. Email marketing, SMS marketing, terms acceptance are separate checkboxes. Never bundled.
  4. Each consent is logged with the exact copy shown at the time (copy_shown field). If we change popup wording, the log still reflects what the user actually agreed to.
  5. Unsubscribe is one click, from every marketing email. Resend's unsubscribe header handles this — verify it's populated.
  6. Data subject requests (SAR under GDPR, PAIA request under POPIA): we need a process. For v1, route any incoming request to a documented internal inbox; the actual process can be operationalised later, but the route must exist.
  7. Privacy notice must disclose cross-border data transfers (SA customer data → UK/EU infra via Shopify/Stripe/Klaviyo). One paragraph, linked from the popup and checkout. Monique will handle the copy; your job is to make sure the link resolves.

If any of these feel like they conflict with a pattern you were going to build, pause and flag. Don't work around them.

Phasing

Phase 1 — Build in staging (2 days)

  • Signup popup with 10% off, unticked granular consent, "no thanks" dismiss
  • Consent log table + write path from popup
  • Discount code generation + exclusion logic (no bundle, no S&S)
  • Tokenised guest-tracking URL + /orders/track page
  • Post-purchase "claim your account" prompt + magic-link activation flow
  • Marketing consent checkbox at checkout (mirrors popup)

Phase 2 — QA (half a day)

  • Guest checkout end-to-end: cart → checkout → pay → confirmation → track (no login required at any step)
  • Claim-account flow: confirmation → email link → set password → account active with order history populated
  • Popup dismissal cookie respected (don't re-show for 30 days)
  • Discount code rejected on bundle + S&S orders (or silently skips those lines)
  • Unsubscribe link works and writes a consent-withdrawn row to the log
  • Privacy notice link resolves from popup + checkout

Phase 3 — Pre-cutover review (half a day)

  • Monique reviews the staging flow end-to-end
  • Legal review if we decide to run one (Monique to confirm)
  • Cutover scheduled for a low-traffic window

Phase 4 — Cutover

  • Swap current logic for new. Current account-creation-at-checkout behaviour retired.
  • Monitor consent log writes, popup submit rate, first-order discount redemption for 48 hours
  • Any issues, we roll back (keep the old flow in place as a feature flag until we're sure)

Open questions back to Monique

Answer these before Phase 1 kicks off — they shape the build:

  1. Flat 10% across all six currencies, or fixed-value per currency (e.g. £5 / $6 / €6 / R100 etc.)? Flat % is operationally simpler; fixed-value is tidier UX.
  2. SMS marketing on the roadmap in 2026? Answer changes whether we build the SMS checkbox placeholder now or skip it.
  3. Subscribe-and-save staying at 15%? The 10% recommendation assumes S&S stays the bigger carrot. If S&S is under review, flag it now.
  4. Bundles going live at launch or post-launch? Affects whether the "bundle SKU exclusion" on the 10% code has any live SKUs to exclude on day one.
  5. Privacy policy copy owner — is Monique writing the cross-border transfer paragraph, or do we need to commission legal copy?

Things that are NOT in scope for this ticket

  • Magic-link / OTP as a login method (i.e. replacing passwords for returning customers). That's the right direction long-term but scoped for a v2 ticket.
  • Google / Apple SSO. Also a v2 — cheap but separate scope.
  • A loyalty programme. Different strategic decision; if we go there, it's a much bigger ticket.
  • SMS marketing infrastructure. Placeholder only in this ticket.
  • A preference centre UI (customer-facing page to update consent). v2. For now, unsubscribe writes to the log and that's enough.

Checklist

  • Read the research doc if curious about why (optional)
  • Answer the open questions above before starting build
  • Phase 1: build in staging
  • Phase 2: QA end-to-end
  • Phase 3: Monique + legal review
  • Phase 4: cutover + 48-hour monitor
  • Keep current flow live until cutover is complete

Why this matters

We're going to lose fewer carts at first-sale conversion, capture more first-time emails than a 5% offer would, and get repeat-purchase accounts without forcing anyone through a reg wall. And we'll do it with a consent architecture that's clean under GDPR, PECR, and POPIA — so Legal can sleep, and when SA volume picks up we're not scrambling to retrofit cross-border disclosures into a flow that's already shipped.

The hardest part isn't the popup or the discount — it's the consent log and the tokenised tracking URL. Those are the infrastructure pieces everything else rides on. Do those two well and the rest is composition.

Issue: KSM-66® logo asset — available for site use
Issue description:
Asset Location: https://dnthefourths.sharepoint.com/sites/ProjectsServaas/Shared%20Documents/Forms/AllItems.aspx?id=%2Fsites%2FProjectsServaas%2FShared%20Documents%2FHealios%20Project%20Assets%2FProducts%2FKSM%2D66&viewid=b74cb911%2D9ed8%2D44b8%2Dbb76%2D5c7bf439f4b0

The Healios has been supplied with the official KSM-66® logo pack for use as a certification badge on the Ashwagandha product page. Logos have been dropped into the Servaas project site in SharePoint. This unblocks the KSM-66® badge requirement in the Ashwagandha site-fix ticket.

Background

Healios is an approved KSM-66® brand partner with logo usage rights under the partnership agreement. The logos can be used publicly on the Ashwagandha product page (and any future Healios products that incorporate KSM-66®), on marketing collateral, and in social/campaign assets. No approval request is needed for each individual use — the brand partner status covers it — but logo integrity must be preserved.

What's available — and what's coming

Currently in the SharePoint folder (ProjectsServaas > Healios Project Assets > Products > KSM-66):

  • KSM-66 Logo(white-vector form).jpeg
  • ksm-66_Logo copy 2.jpg
  • ksm-66_Logo copy.jpg
  • ksm-66_Logo.jpg

⚠ Known issue — format upgrade in flight: all four files are currently JPEG. JPEGs don't support transparent backgrounds, which is a problem for a certification badge that may render on non-white backgrounds. Monique is contacting the team to request:

  • PNG with transparent background (minimum viable for web)
  • SVG vector (ideal — scales cleanly at any size)
  • Full variant set (primary colour, white/reversed, black/single-colour)
  • Official brand guidelines PDF

Expected turnaround: within a few days.

How to proceed in the meantime

  • Build the rendering slot and the certification-badge data model now (per the Certification badges ticket)
  • Use the current JPEGs as placeholders to confirm sizing, placement, and layout look right on the Ashwagandha product page
  • Swap in the PNG / SVG once Monique drops them into the same SharePoint folder
  • Do not ship the JPEGs to production — they'll pick up an unwanted white rectangle background on coloured sections of the page and won't hold up to scrutiny on a trademark logo

Logo usage guidelines

The KSM-66® trademark logo comes with standard third-party-trademark usage rules. Until Ixoreal supplies their full brand guidelines PDF, apply the following as a safe default:

  • Keep the ® symbol in any on-page text reference (e.g. "KSM-66® Ashwagandha Root Extract")
  • Don't stretch, skew, or rotate the logo
  • Don't recolour — use only the provided official colour files
  • Maintain clear space — at minimum 50% of the logo's height as padding on all sides, ideally more
  • Minimum size — don't render smaller than ~60px wide on screen; the logo details become illegible below that
  • Background — place on clean, uncluttered backgrounds (white, neutral, or the Healios navy/gold palette if contrast is adequate)
  • Don't co-mix — don't overlay or merge the KSM-66® logo with the Healios logo; they should sit as separate, distinct marks
  • If a brand guidelines PDF is included in the SharePoint drop, those guidelines override the defaults above

Where the logo appears on-site

Primary use — Ashwagandha product page:

  • As a certification badge in the badges / trust-signals band (see the Certification badges ticket for where that band lives on product pages)
  • Could also appear in the product hero image composite if Monique supplies a design refresh

Acceptance criteria
